title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21
key_entities: IFS Centre for Tax Analysis in Developing Countries (TaxDev)
summary: This report analyzes Ethiopia's import tax expenditures from FY 2018/19 to 2020/21, highlighting their role in promoting investment, technology transfer, and international competitiveness. It details tax expenditure trends as a share of GDP and import value, noting significant variations across commodity groups such as vehicles and animal fats. The analysis also addresses data reliability issues resulting from the transition between customs IT systems during the reporting period.

title: September 2022
summary: September 2022

title: Tax Policy Directorate, Ministry of Finance, Ethiopia
summary: Tax Policy Directorate, Ministry of Finance, Ethiopia

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 2
summary: This document provides the table of contents for the Import Tax Expenditure Report covering the fiscal years 2018/19 to 2020/21, authored by the Ministry of Finance, Ethiopia. It outlines sections on policy rationale, data definitions, and detailed tax expenditure estimates for specific categories like customs duty, excise duty, VAT, and surtax.

title: Preface
summary: Preface

title: Contents
summary: Contents

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 3
summary: This report evaluates Ethiopia's import tax expenditures from FY 2018/19 to FY 2020/21, noting that costs reached ETB 120.7 billion in the final year. Using the revenue forgone method and data from the Ethiopian Customs Commission, it analyzes the fiscal impact of exemptions across VAT, customs duty, excise tax, and surtax to determine if they align with national development goals.

title: Executive summary
summary: Executive summary

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 4
summary: This report section details that import tax expenditures in Ethiopia for FY 2020/21 were largely driven by motor vehicles and cooking oil, which together constituted nearly 40% of the total. While capital goods and 'second schedule' rates represent a small fraction of expenditures, significant growth occurred in ad hoc exemptions and inconsistent surtax applications.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 5
summary: This report provides estimates of federal import tax expenditures in Ethiopia for fiscal years 2018/19 through 2020/21 using shipment-level microdata from the Ethiopian Customs Commission. It analyzes revenue forgone from customs duty, excise, VAT, and surtax by comparing actual collections to a benchmark tax system while excluding withholding tax and domestic taxes.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 7
summary: Tax expenditures in Ethiopia suffer from a lack of transparency because businesses with no tax liability often fail to file returns, hindering comprehensive reporting. This opacity increases risks of corruption and rent-seeking, even though these incentives are intended to transition the nation into an industrial economy.

title: The intended effects of such tax policy measures and incentives are mainly:
summary: The Ethiopian government, through the Ministry of Finance, utilizes tax incentives such as ad hoc exemptions, duty-free imports, and tax holidays to stabilize commodity prices and stimulate investment in the manufacturing and leather sectors. These measures, supported by specific regulations like Proclamation No. 768/2012, aim to strengthen domestic production capacity and accelerate the country's economic development.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 8
summary: This report outlines the methodology for estimating import tax expenditures in Ethiopia using the revenue forgone method, which calculates deviations from a defined benchmark tax system. The benchmark incorporates standard proclamation rates, international agreements, and diplomatic exemptions, while specifically categorizing preferential second schedule rates for manufacturers as tax expenditures.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 9
key_entities: Common Market for Eastern and Southern Africa (COMESA)
summary: Ethiopia provides a 10% customs duty reduction for goods originating from COMESA member states, applying specific benchmark rates based on relevant trade agreements or the 2017 tariff book. The customs duty tax base is determined by the cost, insurance, and freight (CIF) value of the imported items.

title: Customs duty
summary: The Ethiopian import tax system applies customs duty, excise tax, VAT, and surtax sequentially, meaning tax expenditures on early taxes indirectly influence the base and collection of subsequent taxes. While the report attributes these indirect effects to the originating tax, it notes that such expenditures may actually boost domestic revenue by increasing business profits and taxable income.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 10
summary: This section of the Ethiopian import tax expenditure report outlines the governance of Value Added Tax (VAT) under Proclamation No. 285/2002. It explains the invoice-credit system used by registered taxpayers and identifies specific exemptions, including humanitarian aid and financial securities.

title: The benchmark tax system for customs duty is defined as follows:
summary: Customs duty in Ethiopia is regulated by Proclamation No. 859/2014 and is calculated as a percentage of the total duty-paying value, which includes shipment costs, transport charges, and insurance. The applicable rates range from 0% to 35%, with higher taxes levied on final consumer goods and lower rates applied to raw materials and intermediate products.

title: Excise on imports
summary: This section outlines excise import duty exemptions, identifying privileges for international aid organizations like USAID and SIDA as tax expenditures. It also defines benchmark systems for diplomatic personnel under the Vienna Conventions and details a bilateral trade agreement granting Sudan zero-rate customs duty.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 11
summary: This section details the application of surtax in Ethiopia, which is governed by Council of Ministers Regulation No. 133/2007 and typically applied at a 10% flat rate. The Ministry of Finance has implemented various exemptions for basic goods, capital investment items, and specific sectors like petroleum and transportation to reduce the tax burden on essential imports.

title: The benchmark system for excise on imports is defined as follows:
summary: Excise rates for imports are established under Proclamation No. 1186/2020. This tax applies to demand-inelastic products, luxury items, and goods deemed to have negative externalities such as alcohol, tobacco, and motor vehicles.

title: VAT on imports
summary: This section outlines excise tax exemptions and benchmark systems for imports, highlighting privileges for international organizations and diplomatic personnel under the Vienna Conventions. It defines the tax base as the sum of CIF value and customs duty, with rates determined by Proclamation No. 307/2002 and Proclamation No. 1186/2020.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 12
summary:
Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 12
 Ministry of Finance, Ethiopia

title: Surtax on imports
summary: This section outlines the rules for VAT on imports, noting that a 15% benchmark rate applies to the sum of CIF value, customs duty, and excise duty. It distinguishes between standard exemptions under the Vienna Conventions and tax expenditures for international aid organizations like USAID and SIDA, while cautioning that VAT deductibility impacts revenue estimates.

title: The benchmark system for VAT on imports is defined as follows:
summary: This section outlines the benchmark system for VAT on imports, specifically identifying exemptions for essential goods. These include agricultural inputs such as fertilizers and seeds, as well as basic food items like bread, injera, and unprocessed grains.

title: Customs duty tax expenditures are given by
summary: This section outlines the methodology for calculating customs duty tax expenditures in Ethiopia, emphasizing how the sequential nature of taxes like VAT and excise affects the final tax base. It defines the benchmark surtax rate at 10% and distinguishes between taxable expenditures, such as aid for organizations like USAID, and benchmark exemptions under the Vienna Conventions.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 13
summary: This section outlines the formula for calculating the surtax tax base, which incorporates the CIF value along with customs duty, excise tax, and VAT. It specifies that surtax is the final tax applied in the import sequence, resulting in no indirect effects on the calculation of other taxes.

title: The benchmark tax system for surtax on imports is defined as follows:
summary: The benchmark tax system for surtax on imports is defined as follows:

title: Finally, for surtax, the tax expenditure is
summary: This section explains that the tax expenditure for surtax is determined using the VAT tax base, which incorporates import values, customs duties, and excise taxes. It references specific mathematical variables for these calculations and notes that further details are provided in Appendix B.

title: For VAT, the tax expenditure is computed analogously as
summary: This section explains that the VAT tax expenditure is calculated using a method analogous to customs duties, incorporating variables specific to excise taxes. It defines the excise tax base as the import value plus customs duty and directs the reader to Appendix B for further details on variable calculations.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 14
summary: This section notes that a transition between two customs IT systems may have caused shipment records to be duplicated, leading to potential overestimations of tax expenditures for FY 2018/19 and FY 2019/20. These discrepancies, which total billions of ETB, significantly impact the interpretation of fiscal trends and results, although FY 2020/21 estimates remain unaffected.

title: Similarly, excise tax expenditures are computed as
summary: This section outlines the methodology for calculating customs duty tax expenditures arising from exemptions and reduced rates. It defines expenditures as the difference between benchmark and actual duty rates applied to the cost, insurance, and freight (CIF) of imports, while also accounting for indirect effects on other import taxes.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 15
summary: Tax expenditures in Ethiopia increased to 21.3% of total import value and 2.8% of GDP in fiscal year 2020/21, reflecting a 54% nominal growth from the previous year. This expansion significantly outpaced the 18% increase in total import value, driven by notable increases in expenditures for customs duty, excise, VAT, and surtax.

title: The data contain shipment-level information on:
summary: This report utilizes shipment-level microdata from the Ethiopian Customs Commission at the eight-digit HS level for the period between FY 2018/19 and FY 2020/21. The data set was created by merging records from the legacy ASYCUDA++ system and the new electronic Customs Management System (eCMS) following a technological transition.

title: VAT paid;
Customs duty tax expenditure
summary: Customs duty tax expenditure

title: ETB billion
summary: In FY 2020/21, total tax expenditures reached ETB 120.7 billion, primarily driven by forgone VAT on imports and customs duties. While VAT expenditures saw a 26% nominal increase compared to the previous fiscal year, reporting discrepancies occurred during a transition between IT systems such as eCMS. The data further distinguishes between direct and indirect tax effects, noting that surtax expenditures do not impact the collection of other taxes.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 17
summary: This report section details fluctuations in Ethiopian import tax expenditures from FY 2018/19 to 2020/21, noting how government policies like cooking oil exemptions and new excise tax proclamations influenced spending. Vehicle imports and animal or vegetable fats emerged as leading categories for tax expenditures in 2020/21, while the non-capital category consistently represented the largest share of total expenditure. The data, sourced from the Ethiopian Customs Commission, highlights a significant increase in total expenditures reaching 116,724.33 million ETB in the 2020/21 fiscal year.

title: Table 4.1. Tax expenditures by type (in ETB million), FY 2018/19 to FY 2020/21
summary: This section outlines tax expenditures from FY 2018/19 to FY 2020/21, noting a marked increase in the 2020/21 fiscal year. The rise is attributed to higher statutory excise tax rates on vehicles, which subsequently increased the value of capital goods exemptions.

title: Table 4.2. Tax expenditures by broad commodity category (in ETB million), FY 2018/19 to FY 2020/21
summary: This section details tax expenditures across various commodity categories measured in ETB million between the fiscal years 2018/19 and 2020/21. A notable observation within this period is that the import value for animal and vegetable fats grew by more than threefold between 2019/20 and 2020/21.

title: Box 4.1. Tax expenditures estimated using other methods
summary: Current tax expenditure estimates are slightly higher than previous figures but remain generally consistent. The observed discrepancies are primarily attributed to variations in the definition of the benchmark tax system.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 21
summary: This report analyzes tax expenditures in Ethiopia, noting that forgone customs duties reached ETB 38.9 billion in FY 2020/21, representing a 67% increase from the prior year. It highlights methodological discrepancies between various financial reports caused by transitions between the 2012 and 2017 tariff books and the use of different benchmark rates for customs duty, excise, and VAT.

title: Table 4.4. Different estimates of total tax expenditures (in ETB billions)
summary: This section discusses alternative methods for estimating total tax expenditures in Ethiopia, noting that differences arise from how the benchmark tax system is defined. It specifically compares approaches used by the Ethiopian Customs Commission with those from previous Ministry of Finance reports.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 22
summary: The report outlines that customs duty tax expenditures in Ethiopia reached 0.9% of GDP in FY 2020/21, with exemptions becoming increasingly dominant compared to previous years. In FY 2020/21, exemptions accounted for 71% of these expenditures, while indirect effects on other import taxes contributed 27%. The data highlights that customs duty reductions also impact the broader tax base for all other import taxes.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 23
summary: Between FY 2018/19 and FY 2020/21, customs duty tax expenditures in Ethiopia were increasingly dominated by imports that were neither capital goods nor second schedule items, reaching 97% of the total in the final year. This trend was primarily driven by a significant decline in expenditures for second schedule imports, which fell from 7% to 1.6% over the three-year period.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 29
summary: This section analyzes the trends in Ethiopian excise tax expenditures between FY 2018/19 and FY 2020/21, highlighting a dramatic increase in vehicle import expenditures which reached ETB 12.9 billion. While mineral oil and fuel previously dominated expenditures, vehicle imports grew to represent nearly 69% of the total by FY 2020/21, driven largely by non-capital commodity categories. The report utilizes data from the Ethiopian Customs Commission to show how tax expenditure composition has shifted across different commodity types and investment schedules.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 31
summary: Tax expenditures on motor vehicle imports in Ethiopia rose sharply to ETB 23.0 billion in 2020/21, largely due to higher statutory excise tax rates on used vehicles. Although benchmark tax expectations increased significantly, actual collections remained nearly flat, suggesting that 'prohibitive' tax rates influenced importer behavior and skewed revenue foregone estimates.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 32
summary: VAT expenditures in Ethiopia accounted for 1.0% of GDP and 36.9% of total import tax expenditures during the 2020/21 fiscal year. While the share relative to GDP remained stable compared to the previous year, VAT's proportion of overall tax expenditures saw a notable decline from approximately 45% recorded in FY 2019/20.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 34
summary: This report analyzes Ethiopian VAT expenditures from FY 2018/19 to 2020/21, highlighting that machinery and animal or vegetable fats were the most significant commodity groups in the final fiscal year. The data shows substantial fluctuations in expenditure categories like mineral oil and fuel, while the 'non-capital/second schedule' category remains the largest contributor to total VAT expenditure.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 37
summary: Surtax expenditures in Ethiopia are increasingly dominated by shipments outside of capital imports or the second schedule, reaching 97% of the total in FY 2020/21. This shift reflects a significant decline in second schedule imports, which fell from 10% to 1.9% as the government actively phases out these tax privileges.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 38
summary: This report details Ethiopian surtax expenditures from FY 2018/19 to FY 2020/21, highlighting a significant increase in expenditures for animal or vegetable fats to ETB 4.2 billion due to import value growth and ad hoc exemptions. During this period, the expenditure share for non-electrical machinery decreased, while total non-capital expenditures reached ETB 17,896.81 million by the 2020/21 fiscal year.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 39
summary: This section outlines the surtax expenditures for Ethiopia during the 2020/21 fiscal year, categorized by broad commodity groups. The data, sourced from the Ethiopian Customs Commission, highlights that animal or vegetable fats and various machinery types incurred the highest direct effects of surtax expenditure, measured in ETB millions.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 40
summary: The report analyzes Ethiopian import tax expenditures between FY 2018/19 and 2020/21, noting that they rose to ETB 120.7 billion and accounted for 2.8% of GDP in the final year. It recommends expanding analysis to include domestic taxes and calls for regular scrutiny of costly expenditures, particularly those related to motor vehicles and vegetable fats.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 41
summary: The report highlights limitations in shipment-level microdata, which often lack the necessary details to identify causes of tax underpayment. To improve tax expenditure reporting, the document emphasizes the need for higher-quality, more timely data regarding both domestic indirect and direct taxes.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 42
summary: This section examines negative tax expenditures in Ethiopia, which peaked during FY 2018/19 and FY 2019/20 due to the transition between HS 2012 and HS 2017 tariff books. Although these figures are excluded from the report's main estimates by adjusting the benchmark system, they highlight instances where actual tax rates exceeded standard rates due to administrative or recording errors.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 43
summary: This section of the report provides mathematical formulas to calculate the indirect effects of customs tax expenditures on other revenue sources. It specifically details how these expenditures impact excise tax, VAT, and surtax, while categorizing minor terms as higher-order effects.

title: B. Details on the calculation of indirect effects of tax expenditures
summary: This section details the calculation of indirect effects of tax expenditures from fiscal years 2018/19 to 2020/21 based on data from the Ministry of Revenues. It defines 'negative tax expenditures' as cases where actual tax rates exceed the benchmark system rates and incorporates indirect effects while assuming no duplicate reporting within customs IT systems.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 44
summary: This section describes two methods for calculating import tax expenditures: one that includes direct and indirect knock-on effects and an alternative measure comparing benchmark versus actual tax collections. While total expenditures remain the same, the alternative measure redistributes figures, resulting in lower customs duty expenditures and higher VAT and surtax expenditures compared to the primary methodology.

title: C. Alternative measures of tax expenditures
summary: This section explains a methodology for calculating indirect effects to ensure consistency in tax expenditure reporting. By using this approach, the sum of individual expenditures aligns with the total difference between the benchmark import tax and the actual amount paid on a shipment.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 45
summary: The Ethiopian Customs Commission transitioned from ASYCUDA++ to the eCMS IT system between FY 2017/18 and FY 2019/20, leading to minor data discrepancies and potential double-counting of shipments. Despite these challenges and some incomplete records, the customs microdata successfully accounted for 96% to 97% of the actual import tax revenue collected by the Ministry of Revenues.

High-expenditure categories include vehicles, base metals, and electrical machinery, while sectors such as fertilizer and aircraft recorded zero expenditure. The statistics were derived from calculations using data provided by the Ethiopian Customs Commission.

title: Table F.3. VAT expenditure by commodity category in (ETB million), FY 2018/19 to FY 2020/21
summary: This section details VAT expenditure across various commodity categories in ETB million for the fiscal years 2018/19 through 2020/21. summary: This section details surtax expenditure across various commodity categories in Ethiopia for the fiscal years 2018/19 to 2020/21, measured in ETB million.
